worth_it_explanation: Amazon DSP's three bid strategy options (spend as efficiently as possible, maximize a performance goal, and fixed bid) each make a fundamentally different tradeoff, and selecting the wrong one for the line item's objective wastes budget. A line item optimizing toward awareness at the lowest cost does not belong on a purchase-goal order. A target cost set below what the inventory actually clears throttles delivery and leaves budget unspent. These checks take only a few minutes in the Amazon DSP console and surface configuration mismatches that depress performance without appearing in any single metric.
intro: Amazon DSP offers three bid strategies at the line item level: 'Spend as efficiently as possible' (dynamic CPM aimed at the lowest achievable cost for the chosen goal), 'Maximize a goal' with an optional target cost, and 'Fixed bid' (a static CPM that bypasses dynamic optimization). Choosing among them correctly requires matching the strategy to the line item's objective, then setting any target cost at a level the available inventory can actually support. When the strategy and the goal are misaligned, or when a target cost is set so low that the line item loses most auctions it enters, the campaign underdelivers without a clear signal in the Amazon DSP reporting columns that explain why.

audit_checks.title: Bid strategy does not match the line item's optimization goal
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A bid strategy that conflicts with the line item's goal sends the optimization engine toward a metric that is not the one the campaign needs. Spend accrues against an efficient but irrelevant signal while the goal metric remains unmoved.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP's bid strategy setting determines how the auction algorithm allocates spend across the impressions a line item is eligible to win. 'Spend as efficiently as possible' tells the algorithm to win impressions at the lowest cost for the delivery goal the line item targets, whether that is viewable impressions, click-throughs, or detail page views (DPVs). 'Maximize a goal' tells the algorithm to optimize toward a specific outcome and optionally stay within a target cost per that outcome. 'Fixed bid' bypasses dynamic optimization entirely and bids the same CPM on every eligible impression.

Each strategy fits a different use case. 'Spend as efficiently as possible' suits line items where delivery volume matters more than a specific outcome cost, such as awareness or reach line items where the goal is impression coverage at low cost. 'Maximize a goal' suits line items where a downstream outcome, such as a DPV, an Add-to-Cart event, or a purchase, is the primary objective and where the advertiser is willing to pay more per impression to reach users more likely to convert. 'Fixed bid' suits line items in managed-service deals or private marketplace (PMP) buys where a negotiated price applies, not programmatic optimization.

The mismatch to watch for is a 'spend as efficiently as possible' strategy on a line item whose parent order is built for purchase or conversion outcomes. That strategy will minimize CPM, not maximize purchases, so the line item serves to cheaper audiences rather than to the higher-intent audiences that conversion goals require. The inverse mismatch, a 'maximize a goal' strategy on a broad awareness line item with a very aggressive target cost, restricts the line item to a narrow pool of users who happen to have a high predicted conversion probability, which may not be the right pool for a broad reach objective.

A few signs point to this check applying:
  • A line item with a purchase or DPV goal is using 'Spend as efficiently as possible' as its strategy
  • A broad awareness line item is using 'Maximize a goal' with a tight target cost that limits its reach
  • A programmatic display line item not in a fixed-price deal is set to 'Fixed bid', bypassing dynamic optimization
You may want to read the bid strategy setting alongside the order goal and the line item's reported goal metric before drawing a conclusion.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers whose goal is Amazon Standard Identification Number (ASIN) purchases or Add-to-Cart events, 'Maximize a goal' with a DPV or purchase goal is the strategy best suited to conversion line items. 'Spend as efficiently as possible' will minimize CPM on those line items, which may surface impressions from audiences with low purchase intent. You may want to confirm that any line item targeting in-market or remarketing audiences with a purchase goal is set to 'Maximize a goal' rather than cost-efficient delivery.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers driving off-Amazon conversions tracked through the Amazon Ad Tag pixel, 'Maximize a goal' targeting a cost per action or click may be appropriate for conversion-focused line items. 'Spend as efficiently as possible' suits brand-awareness activity where impression volume matters. You may want to confirm the strategy reflects which type of outcome the line item is built to drive.

learn_more.source_title: Bid strategies for Amazon DSP self-service orders

investigate.intro: For each line item, note the bid strategy setting and compare it against the parent order's goal and the line item's reported performance on its optimization metric.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the Amazon DSP console and navigate to the Advertiser whose campaigns you are auditing.
For each Order, note the campaign goal type shown in the order settings.
Open each Line Item under the order and navigate to the bid settings section. Record the bid strategy in use: 'Spend as efficiently as possible', 'Maximize a goal', or 'Fixed bid'.

Compare the bid strategy to the order goal. Flag any line item with a purchase or DPV goal that is using 'Spend as efficiently as possible'. Flag any awareness or reach line item that is using 'Maximize a goal' with a restrictive target cost.

In the line item's performance report, note whether the primary goal metric (such as DPV rate, Add-to-Cart rate, or purchase rate) is producing results that justify the strategy. A low goal-metric rate on a 'Maximize a goal' line item may indicate the strategy is set correctly but the target cost needs review rather than the strategy itself.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per line item, the order goal, the bid strategy in use, and whether the strategy is appropriate for the goal type. A line item with a purchase or DPV goal using 'Maximize a goal' passes. A line item with a purchase or DPV goal using 'Spend as efficiently as possible' is a fail recorded with the order name, line item name, and the specific mismatch. A programmatic display line item not in a fixed-price deal using 'Fixed bid' is a fail recorded with a note that dynamic optimization is disabled.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the order goal and each line item's bid strategy setting above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more line items in {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP campaigns are using a bid strategy that does not match the line item's optimization goal. When the strategy is set to minimize cost-per-impression on a line item whose goal is purchases or detail page views, the algorithm optimizes for cheap inventory rather than high-intent audiences, and the goal metric goes unimproved. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each line item's bid strategy alongside the parent order's goal, and switching conversion-focused line items to 'Maximize a goal' with a realistic target cost. For awareness and reach line items, 'Spend as efficiently as possible' is the appropriate setting.

audit_checks.title: Target cost is set below what the inventory clears, throttling delivery
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A target cost that is below the market rate for the audience and supply type causes the line item to lose most auctions it enters. Budget goes unspent during the flight, and the campaign does not reach the audiences it was designed to reach.

educate.base:
When a line item uses the 'Maximize a goal' bid strategy, the Amazon DSP console lets the advertiser enter a target cost, for example a target cost per DPV or a target cost per acquisition. The algorithm treats this as a soft ceiling: it attempts to deliver the goal at or below the specified cost, and it reduces bids or becomes more selective when achieving the target requires paying more than the specified amount.

Setting a target cost below what the available inventory actually requires does not make the line item more efficient. It makes the line item more conservative. The algorithm rejects more auctions than it enters in order to stay within the constraint. At the extreme, a target cost that is far below market rate produces a line item that barely spends, reports a technically low cost per outcome on the few impressions it wins, but delivers so few impressions that the reported outcomes are also very few.

Amazon's optimization engine needs a certain volume of signal to learn which audiences and placements drive the goal metric. A throttled line item collects signal slowly, which further delays the optimization from improving. The result looks like low cost-per-goal in reporting but represents a line item that is not working at the scale the campaign needs.

A few signs point to a target cost that may be too restrictive:
  • The line item's daily spend is well below its daily budget across multiple consecutive days
  • Impression volume is low relative to the audience size the line item targets
  • The reported cost per goal is at or just below the target, but total goal events are far below what the budget would imply at that cost
You may want to compare the target cost against the account's historical cost per goal for similar line items, or against Amazon DSP's delivery forecasting tool, before concluding the target needs adjustment. Some low-spend days reflect flight scheduling rather than bid throttling.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers optimizing toward DPVs or purchases, the realistic target cost varies considerably by ASIN category, audience segment, and time of year. An in-market audience for a high-consideration product category commands a higher cost per DPV than a broad run-of-network audience. You may want to look at the historical cost per DPV for the account and set the target cost within 20 to 30 percent of that figure to give the algorithm room to operate.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers tracking off-Amazon conversions, the Amazon Ad Tag pixel provides the signal the 'Maximize a goal' strategy optimizes toward. A target cost per acquisition set without reference to the actual conversion rate the pixel records will either throttle the line item or allow it to overspend. You may want to establish a baseline conversion rate from any prior Amazon DSP flights before setting a target cost.

learn_more.source_title: Bid strategies for Amazon DSP self-service orders

investigate.intro: Check each line item using 'Maximize a goal' for signs that its target cost is below market rate, by comparing daily spend to budget, impression volume to audience size, and reported cost per goal to historical benchmarks.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the Amazon DSP console and filter to line items using the 'Maximize a goal' bid strategy.
For each such line item, note the target cost setting, the line item's daily budget, and the actual daily spend over the past 7 to 14 days.
A daily spend that is consistently 50 percent or less of the daily budget, with no apparent scheduling or flight-date explanation, is a signal worth investigating further.

Open the line item's performance report and note the total impressions delivered relative to the audience size. A large audience with low impression volume suggests the algorithm is passing on most eligible auctions.

If prior flights exist in the account for similar line items, note the historical cost per goal and compare it to the current target cost. A target cost set at half the historical rate is unlikely to clear enough inventory to spend the budget.

amazon_marketing_cloud.label: Amazon Marketing Cloud
steps: If Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC) access is available, run a custom query comparing the line item's cost per goal to its impression win rate over the flight window.
A win rate that has declined since the target cost was set, or that is lower than comparable line items without a target cost, points to the target cost restricting auction participation.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per 'Maximize a goal' line item, the target cost, the average daily spend as a percentage of the daily budget, and whether impression volume appears proportionate to audience size. A line item spending more than 80 percent of its daily budget with impression volume consistent with its audience passes. A line item spending less than 50 percent of its daily budget across multiple non-scheduling days, with low impression volume relative to audience size, is a fail recorded with the line item name, target cost, and the spend and impression pattern observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the target cost setting, daily spend pattern, and impression volume for each 'Maximize a goal' line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more line items in {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP campaigns are consistently underdelivering against their daily budgets, with impression volume that is disproportionately low for the audience sizes targeted. The target cost on these line items appears to be below what the available inventory clears, causing the algorithm to pass on most auctions it enters in order to stay within the constraint. Budget goes unspent and the campaign does not reach the audiences it was designed to reach. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the target cost for each underdelivering line item against historical cost-per-goal figures for similar campaigns in the account, and adjusting the target cost to a range the inventory can realistically clear. Starting without a target cost and letting the line item spend freely for a short window to establish a baseline cost per goal is one approach before reintroducing a constraint.

audit_checks.title: Fixed bid is in use on a programmatic line item where dynamic optimization would apply
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Fixed bid disables the signal-driven optimization that Amazon DSP's algorithm performs when it has latitude to adjust bids. The line item bids the same amount on every eligible impression regardless of the impression's predicted value for the goal, which reduces the quality of the inventory the line item wins.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP's 'Fixed bid' strategy sets a static CPM for every auction the line item enters. This is the appropriate setting when a line item is running against a private marketplace (PMP) deal or a direct-buy where a specific CPM has been agreed upon with the supply partner. In those contexts, the deal already determines which inventory the line item accesses, and variable bidding is irrelevant.

On a programmatic line item buying open or Amazon-owned inventory without a deal constraint, 'Fixed bid' bypasses the value-based bidding that the other two strategies use. Instead of raising bids on impressions predicted to be high-value for the line item's goal and lowering them on impressions predicted to be low-value, 'Fixed bid' bids the same CPM on everything. The algorithm does not learn which impressions deliver the goal better; it simply bids uniformly across the inventory pool.

The practical effect is that the line item may win more impressions than a 'Maximize a goal' strategy would win. It is not passing on low-predicted-value inventory the way dynamic optimization would. But those impressions are less likely to produce the goal metric, so the cost per goal can run higher than it would be under dynamic optimization. The line item looks active in terms of impression volume while the goal metric underperforms.

The question to ask is whether the line item has a deal ID attached. If it does, 'Fixed bid' is likely intentional. If it does not, the setting may have been left at a default or carried over from a prior campaign that did have a deal constraint.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, 'Fixed bid' on a display line item targeting in-market audiences for a specific product category passes on Amazon's ability to weight bids toward users with higher DPV or purchase intent. You may want to confirm whether any non-deal programmatic line item targeting an in-market or remarketing audience is using 'Fixed bid', and whether switching to 'Maximize a goal' would improve the cost per DPV.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers, the same principle applies: 'Fixed bid' on a programmatic line item targeting audiences based on Amazon shopping signals removes the algorithm's ability to prioritize higher-intent impressions. You may want to confirm the 'Fixed bid' setting is tied to a deal ID before concluding it is intentional.

learn_more.source_title: Bid strategies for Amazon DSP self-service orders

investigate.intro: Identify programmatic line items using 'Fixed bid' and check whether a PMP deal ID or direct-buy justifies the static CPM setting.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the Amazon DSP console and filter line items by bid strategy to find those using 'Fixed bid'.
For each 'Fixed bid' line item, navigate to the supply settings and check whether a deal ID is associated with the line item.
If a deal ID is present, the fixed bid is likely intentional and the check passes for that line item.

If no deal ID is present and the line item is buying programmatic inventory, note the line item name, the fixed CPM set, and the goal the line item is expected to drive. Record it as a candidate for strategy review.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per 'Fixed bid' line item, whether a PMP deal ID is attached. A 'Fixed bid' line item with a deal ID passes. A 'Fixed bid' line item with no deal ID and a performance goal (DPV, Add-to-Cart, purchase, or conversion) is a fail recorded with the line item name, the fixed CPM in use, and the goal the line item targets.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each 'Fixed bid' line item, whether a deal ID is attached, and the line item's goal above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} one or more programmatic line items in {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP campaigns using a fixed bid without an associated private marketplace deal to justify the static CPM. Fixed bid disables the value-based optimization Amazon DSP performs when it can vary bids based on an impression's predicted contribution to the line item's goal. On programmatic inventory without a deal constraint, this means the line item bids identically on high-intent and low-intent impressions alike, which raises the cost per goal compared to what dynamic optimization would produce. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each fixed-bid programmatic line item, and switching any line item without a deal ID to 'Maximize a goal' or 'Spend as efficiently as possible' so the algorithm can differentiate between impressions by predicted value.
